title: Double page scrapbook spread
subtitle: Composing a two-page scrapbook layout with photos, journalling, and embellishment
excerpt: A double-page spread uses both pages of an open scrapbook as a single design field. The pages share a visual theme, a title, and often a colour palette. Composing a spread involves deciding where the photos go, how much journalling space to reserve, and how embellishments fit without overwhelming the photographs.
sourceNotes: Double-page scrapbook layouts are a contemporary craft technique. The design and composition principles described here draw on graphic design fundamentals and are a synthesis of well-established page layout practices.

content.text: A double-page spread works as a single composition. The key design decisions are where to place the
, how to lead the eye across both pages using a
, and how much space to reserve for the

. The spine of the album divides the two pages; elements placed at or across the spine can tie the spread together, but anything that crosses the spine exactly down the centre is risky because the binding may obscure it.

items.name: Scrapbook album (standard 30 x 30 cm or A4)
Printed photographs, sized and cut to intended final dimensions
Patterned papers and cardstock for matting and background
Adhesive (PVA, glue stick, or foam adhesive squares for dimensional mounting)
Fine liner or journalling pen for writing captions and notes
Craft knife, ruler, and cutting mat for trimming papers and photos

content.text: Choose one photograph as the focal point: the largest, most important, or most visually striking image. Place this on the spread first, off-centre slightly. It does not have to be on the left page.
Arrange the remaining photos around the focal point without gluing. Try placing the second-largest photo diagonally opposite (top-left vs. bottom-right) to create movement across the spread.
Reserve a rectangular block for the journalling. This area should be blank; photographs should not overlap it. Mark it lightly in pencil.
Place the title across the top of the spread or on the left page. The title should be larger than the journalling text but smaller than the focal photograph.
Mat the focal photograph on a piece of cardstock 4 to 6 mm larger on each side. The mat adds a border and lifts the photo visually.
Glue background papers first, then the mats, then the photos, then embellishments. Work from the bottom of the pile upward so each layer sits on top of what was glued before it.
Write or stamp the journalling into the reserved block last, after all glued elements are dry.
